concepts.label: Vectors and data frames
concepts.definition: R stores data in vectors (single-type sequences) and data frames (tables of columns that can each hold a different type).

concepts.label: NA handling
concepts.definition: R represents missing values with NA, which propagates through calculations unless explicitly filtered or handled with functions like is.na() or na.rm.

concepts.label: Factors for categories
concepts.definition: Factors are R's way of storing categorical data as labeled levels rather than plain text or numbers.

concepts.label: The pipe operator
concepts.definition: The pipe (%>% or |>) passes the result of one expression as the first argument to the next, letting you chain operations in readable order.

concepts.label: Filtering and selecting rows/columns
concepts.definition: dplyr's filter() keeps rows matching a condition and select() keeps chosen columns, forming the basic building blocks of data manipulation.

concepts.label: Creating columns with mutate
concepts.definition: dplyr's mutate() adds or changes columns in a data frame by computing new values from existing ones.

concepts.label: Grouped summaries
concepts.definition: dplyr's group_by() combined with summarise() computes aggregate statistics like means or counts separately for each group in the data.

concepts.label: ggplot2 grammar of graphics
concepts.definition: ggplot2 builds plots by layering data, aesthetic mappings, and geometric objects according to a consistent grammar of graphics.

items.stem: An analyst creates a vector with c(1, 2, "three", 4) in R. What is the resulting class of every element in this vector?
options: Character, since coercion favors the most flexible type
Numeric, because c() ignores the quoted string
A list containing both numeric and character values
Integer, because R stores mixed vectors as integers
items.explanation: R vectors hold only one type; combining numeric and character values forces coercion to character, the most flexible type that can represent all elements.

grounding.source: Hadley Wickham, Advanced R: Vectors chapter
grounding.note: Explains that atomic vectors coerce mixed types to the most flexible common type.

items.stem: A numeric column in a data frame contains one NA value. An analyst runs mean(df$sales) with no additional arguments. What is returned?
options: NA, because arithmetic involving NA propagates by default
The mean of the non-missing values only
Zero, since R treats NA as zero in numeric calculations
An error stopping the function from executing
items.explanation: mean() propagates NA unless na.rm = TRUE is set; option B describes the behavior only after adding that argument.

grounding.note: Base R help page states na.rm defaults to FALSE, so missing values propagate.

items.stem: In dplyr, an analyst writes filter(df, region == "West", sales > 100). How does R combine these two conditions?
options: With logical AND, keeping rows where both conditions are true
With logical OR, keeping rows where either condition is true
Sequentially, applying only the second condition to remaining rows
As separate outputs, returning two data frames
items.explanation: Comma-separated arguments to filter() are combined with AND; option B confuses this with OR logic, which requires the | operator instead.

grounding.note: Documents that multiple conditions passed as separate arguments are combined with &.

items.stem: An analyst runs df %>% group_by(department) %>% summarise(avg_pay = mean(pay)). How many rows appear in the result if there are 5 distinct departments?
options: 5 rows, one summary row per department
The same number of rows as the original data frame
1 row containing the overall average across all departments
5 rows plus one additional total row
items.explanation: summarise() collapses each group to a single row, so 5 departments yield 5 rows; option B confuses this with mutate(), which preserves the original row count.

grounding.note: States that summarise returns one row for each combination of grouping variables.

items.stem: An analyst writes ggplot(df, aes(x = height, y = weight, color = "blue")) + geom_point(). What actually happens to the point colors?
options: Points are mapped to a single-category color scale labeled "blue"
All points are rendered in blue, matching the string literal
The plot fails to render because color cannot be a string
Points are colored by height, since color defaults to the x variable
items.explanation: Placing color = "blue" inside aes() treats it as a variable mapping, producing a single-category legend rather than a fixed color; setting color outside aes() would give blue points.

grounding.note: Explains that expressions inside aes() are treated as data mappings, not fixed aesthetic values.

items.stem: An analyst imports survey data where a satisfaction column contains values Low, Medium, and High stored as a factor. They run mean(df$satisfaction) directly. What happens?
options: R returns NA with a warning, since factors are not treated as numeric
R computes the mean using the underlying integer codes for each factor level
R automatically converts the level labels to numbers before computing the average
R throws an error immediately, halting execution before any output is produced
items.explanation: mean() on a factor issues a warning that the argument is not numeric or logical and returns NA; it does not silently average the internal integer codes.

grounding.note: The base R help page notes mean.default warns and returns NA for non-numeric, non-logical arguments such as factors.

items.stem: An analyst writes df %>% lm(y ~ x, data = .) to fit a regression using the pipe operator. Why is the data = . argument needed here?
options: The pipe defaults to the first argument; the dot moves df to data
lm() cannot accept a data frame passed through the pipe under any circumstance
The pipe operator requires a dot placeholder in every function call it is used in
Writing data = . converts df into a formula object before the model is fit
items.explanation: By default %>% inserts the left-hand value as the first argument; lm() expects a formula first and data second, so the dot explicitly places df in the data argument.

grounding.note: Documents that a dot placeholder can redirect the piped value to an argument position other than the first.

items.stem: An analyst runs df %>% mutate(bonus = ifelse(sales > 1000, 0.1 * sales, 0)). What does this add to the resulting data frame?
options: A new column bonus with a computed value for every row, based on the condition
A single bonus value appended as one extra row at the bottom of the data
A filtered data frame keeping only rows where sales exceed 1000
An error, since mutate() cannot evaluate conditional logic such as ifelse()
items.explanation: mutate() adds a column computed row-by-row for the full data frame; option C confuses this with filter(), which removes rows instead of adding a column.

grounding.note: States that mutate() adds new columns while preserving all existing rows, computed per row using the supplied expression.

items.stem: An analyst runs df$total <- df$price * df$quantity, where price has 10 values and quantity has 5 values. What happens under R's recycling rule?
options: R recycles quantity to match price's length, since 10 divides evenly by 5
R throws an error immediately because the two vectors have different lengths
R truncates price down to the first five values before multiplying
R fills the remaining values in quantity with NA once it is exhausted
items.explanation: R's recycling rule repeats the shorter vector without a warning when its length divides evenly into the longer one; a warning only appears when it does not divide evenly.

grounding.source: CRAN: An Introduction to R, section on vector arithmetic
grounding.note: Describes the recycling rule where shorter vectors are repeated to match the length of longer ones in arithmetic operations.

items.stem: An analyst runs df %>% group_by(region) %>% mutate(region_avg = mean(sales)). How does this differ from replacing mutate() with summarise() in the same pipeline?
options: mutate() adds region_avg to every row; summarise() collapses each region into one row
mutate() collapses each region to one summary row; summarise() keeps every row intact
Both functions produce identical output once the data has been grouped by region
mutate() ignores the grouping and calculates one overall average across all rows
items.explanation: Grouped mutate() computes region_avg per group but retains all original rows, while summarise() reduces each group to a single output row; option B reverses these behaviors.

grounding.note: Explains that grouped mutate() preserves row count while grouped summarise() returns one row per group.

items.stem: An analyst runs a <- c(10, 20, 30); b <- c(1, 2); then computes a + b in R. What is the result?
options: c(11, 22, 31), along with a warning about mismatched vector lengths
An error stopping execution because the vectors have different lengths
c(11, 22, NA), with the unmatched element filled in as missing
c(11, 22), with the extra element silently dropped
items.explanation: R recycles the shorter vector b to match a's length, cycling 1, 2, 1. Since 3 is not a multiple of 2, R issues a warning but still returns a full-length result rather than an error or NA.

grounding.note: Describes vector recycling rules applied during elementwise arithmetic operations.

items.stem: A data frame df has a column score containing some NA values. An analyst runs filter(df, score == NA) to find the missing rows. What does this return?
options: An empty data frame, since comparisons with NA return NA, not TRUE
All rows where score is missing, since == NA matches missing values
An error, because R does not allow == comparisons with NA
All rows of df, since NA is treated as matching everything
items.explanation: Any comparison to NA yields NA, not TRUE or FALSE, and filter() only retains rows where the condition is TRUE. Finding missing values requires is.na(score) instead of == NA.

grounding.note: Explains that NA propagates through comparison operators rather than matching itself.

items.stem: An analyst builds x <- factor(c("10", "20", "30")) and runs as.numeric(x) to recover the original numbers. What is returned?
options: c(1, 2, 3), the factor's internal integer codes
c(10, 20, 30), the original numeric values recovered
c(NA, NA, NA), since factors cannot convert to numeric
c("10", "20", "30"), unchanged as character text values
items.explanation: as.numeric() on a factor returns the underlying integer level codes, not the printed labels. Recovering the original numbers requires as.numeric(as.character(x)) instead.

grounding.note: Describes factors as integer codes paired with a levels attribute for display.

items.stem: An analyst runs df2 <- df %>% mutate(x = x * 2) %>% filter(x > 10). After this line executes, what is true of the original object df?
options: df is unchanged, since the pipe assigns its result only to df2
df now has an added x column, doubled in place
df is reduced to only rows where x is greater than 10
df and df2 become linked, so later changes to df2 also affect df
items.explanation: dplyr verbs and %>% do not modify objects in place; each step returns a new value, and only the final result is assigned to df2, leaving df untouched.

grounding.note: dplyr verbs return new data frames rather than mutating inputs by reference.

items.stem: A data frame has one row per region with a column total_sales already summed. An analyst runs ggplot(df, aes(x = region, y = total_sales)) + geom_bar(). What do the resulting bar heights show?
options: The count of rows per region, not the total_sales values
The total_sales values, exactly as mapped in the aes() call
The average of total_sales values within each region
An error, since geom_bar cannot accept a y aesthetic
items.explanation: geom_bar()'s default stat is "count", which tallies rows per x value regardless of any y mapping. Plotting total_sales directly requires stat = "identity" or geom_col().

grounding.note: Documents geom_bar's default stat = "count" versus geom_col's stat = "identity".

items.stem: An analyst runs summary_df <- df[, 'sales'] on a data frame with multiple columns, expecting a one-column data frame. Instead, the result prints without column headers. Why?
options: Single-bracket indexing with one column name drops to a vector by default
The column name must be quoted with backticks, not quotes
R automatically converts the selected column to a factor, and factors print without column headers
df[, 'sales'] always returns a list, and lists never print column headers
items.explanation: By default, `[` drops to a plain vector when subsetting a data frame to a single column; this is unrelated to quoting style or factor conversion. Use drop = FALSE or df['sales'] to keep a one-column data frame.

grounding.note: Documents the drop argument and default dimension-dropping behavior when subsetting to a single column.

items.stem: A data frame df has some NA values in column x. An analyst runs filter(df, x == NA) intending to keep rows where x is missing. What is the result?
options: Zero rows are returned, because comparisons with NA evaluate to NA, not TRUE
All rows with NA in x are returned, since dplyr treats == NA as a missing-value test
An error is raised because NA cannot appear on the right side of ==
All rows are returned, including those where x is not missing
items.explanation: Any comparison involving NA, including x == NA, evaluates to NA rather than TRUE or FALSE, and filter() only keeps rows where the condition is TRUE. is.na(x) is needed to test for missingness.

grounding.note: Explains that comparisons with NA return NA rather than a logical value.

items.stem: An analyst creates satisfaction <- factor(c('Medium','Low','High','Low')) without specifying levels, then plots it. The bars appear in the order High, Low, Medium. Why?
options: factor() sorts levels alphabetically by default, not by any logical order
ggplot2 always sorts bars by descending frequency
factor() orders levels by the order values first appear in the vector
ggplot2 sorts categorical axes alphabetically, independent of any factor level order
items.explanation: Without an explicit levels argument, factor() assigns levels alphabetically by default, which is why High sorts before Low and Medium. ggplot2 uses the factor's level order for the axis; it does not independently sort alphabetically or by frequency.

grounding.source: R documentation for the factor() function
grounding.note: States that levels default to the sorted unique values of the input when not specified.

items.stem: An analyst runs df %>% aggregate(x ~ group), expecting df to fill the data argument of aggregate(). The call throws an error. What is the issue?
options: The pipe inserts df into aggregate()'s first argument slot, displacing the data argument
The pipe automatically matches df to aggregate()'s data argument by name, regardless of position
aggregate() cannot be used together with the pipe operator under any circumstance
The formula x ~ group must be wrapped in quotes before it can be piped
items.explanation: The pipe always inserts the left-hand side into the first argument position, not by matching argument names. Since aggregate()'s first argument is not data, df lands in the wrong slot. Use data = . to place it correctly.

grounding.source: R for Data Science (Wickham & Grolemund), chapter on pipes
grounding.note: Explains that %>% inserts the left-hand side as the first argument unless a placeholder is used.

items.stem: A data frame has one row per product with a column total_units already summed. An analyst plots ggplot(df, aes(x = product, y = total_units)) + geom_bar(). The bar heights are wrong. Why?
options: geom_bar() by default counts rows per x value, ignoring the y values
geom_bar() requires the data to be sorted by y before plotting
geom_bar() can only plot categorical variables, not numeric totals
geom_bar() automatically log-transforms the y axis for count-like data
items.explanation: geom_bar()'s default stat is 'count', which tallies rows per x category rather than plotting the supplied y values. geom_col(), or stat = 'identity', should be used for pre-summarized data.

grounding.note: States that geom_bar() uses stat_count by default while geom_col() uses the y values directly.
